items.question_text: Which FAA Order contains complete details of TBM, traffic management initiatives and programs as referenced by 11-1-1?
items.explanation: Per the note at 11-1-1, complete details of TBM, traffic management initiatives and programs can be found in FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.
FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 11, Traffic Management.
FAA Order JO 7610.4, Special Operations.
FAA Order JO 3120.4, Air Traffic Technical Training.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-1, the traffic management mission is described as balancing demand with capacity. What is that balance meant to ensure?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1a, the mission is to balance air traffic demand with system capacity to ensure the maximum efficient utilization of the NAS.

choices.text: the maximum efficient utilization of the NAS
maximum efficient utilization of the NAS

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, the TBFM duties assigned to ARTCCs and TRACONs begin with what qualifier?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2d and e, the ARTCC and TRACON TBFM duties apply 'unless otherwise coordinated.' The ATCT duties in paragraph f carry the same qualifier. This means the standard duties apply by default but can be modified through coordination.

choices.text: Only when TBFM metering is actively in progress.
Unless otherwise coordinated.
When authorized by the STMCIC for the current shift.
Only during periods of peak demand as determined by TMU.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, which EDST-related duty is shared by the STMCIC, OS/CIC, and personnel providing air traffic services?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a5/b6/c5, all three roles must perform assigned actions in the event of an EDST outage or degradation, in accordance with JO 7210.3 and facility directive. Additionally, per a4/b5/c4, all three are responsible for EDST data entries where authorized.

choices.text: Initiating EDST outage declarations for the facility.
Reconfiguring EDST airspace boundaries without higher-level authorization.
Performing assigned actions in the event of an EDST outage or degradation per JO 7210.3 and facility directive.
Disabling EDST automation for sectors when traffic complexity exceeds thresholds.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2a, the STMCIC must ensure changes to TBM operations and TMIs are implemented in a _____ manner.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a6, the STMCIC must ensure changes to TBM operations and TMIs are implemented in a timely manner.

choices.text: timely

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2b, the OS/CIC must coordinate with the TMU and personnel providing air traffic services to develop what?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2b2, the OS/CIC must coordinate with the TMU and personnel providing air traffic services to develop appropriate TBM operations or TMIs for sectors and airports in their area of responsibility.

choices.text: Staffing schedules for the upcoming shift based on projected demand.
Appropriate TBM operations or TMIs for sectors and airports in their area of responsibility.
Runway configuration plans in coordination with the ATCT.
EDST airspace element definitions for the next software update cycle.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2c, personnel providing air traffic services must keep the OS/CIC and TMU apprised of what?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2c2, personnel must keep the OS/CIC and TMU apprised of situations or circumstances that may cause congestion or delays.

choices.text: Every deviation from a metering time regardless of operational significance.
Only weather phenomena that require an amended TAF.
Situations or circumstances that may cause congestion or delays.
Routine shift-change information only, because congestion is a TMU responsibility.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, for ATCTs, TBFM equipment monitoring improves what?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2f1, ATCTs must monitor TBFM equipment to improve situational awareness for a system approach to TBM operations.

choices.text: situational awareness

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, both ARTCCs and TRACONs must monitor arrival flow for potential metering actions or changes and, if necessary, initiate coordination with _____ to discuss the change to the metering plan.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2d2 and e2, both ARTCCs and TRACONs must monitor arrival flow and, if necessary, initiate coordination with all facilities to discuss the change to the metering plan.

choices.text: all facilities

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, when STMCIC ensures that TBM operations and TMIs are carried out, which group of personnel must do the carrying out?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a3, the STMCIC ensures that TBM operations and TMIs are carried out by personnel providing traffic management services. Per 11-1-2b4, the OS/CIC ensures they are carried out by personnel providing air traffic services. This distinction matters by role.

choices.text: Personnel providing air traffic services only, regardless of which supervisor is ensuring enforcement.
Personnel providing traffic management services (STMCIC), and personnel providing air traffic services (OS/CIC).
The TMU and ATCT only, since those are the primary delivery points for TBM operations.
All facility personnel regardless of whether they are on position or in training.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, for TRACONs, the duty to schedule internal departures is qualified by what requirement?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2e3, TRACONs must schedule internal departures in accordance with specific written procedures and agreements developed with overlying ARTCCs and adjacent facilities. It is not a generic scheduling authority.

choices.text: May be done at the discretion of the TRACON supervisor without coordinating with any external facility.
Only applies when the ATCT has requested departure scheduling support from the TRACON.
Must be in accordance with specific written procedures and agreements developed with overlying ARTCCs and adjacent facilities.
Must follow the same departure release window as ATCTs, from 2 prior to 1 after.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, an OS/CIC can defer reviewing active TMIs in their area until the next operational briefing.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2b3, the OS/CIC must continuously review TBM operations and TMIs affecting their area of responsibility and coordinate with TMU for extensions, revisions, or cancellations. Deferring until the next briefing would violate the continuous-review requirement.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, when the TBFM delay countdown timer has significant jumps that prevent reliable metering-time accuracy, TMIs such as MIT or MINIT may be used between _____ aircraft to assist delay absorption until stability resumes.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b1, TMIs may be used between those aircraft to assist in delay absorption until stability resumes. The TMIs apply to the specific aircraft affected by DCT instability, not the entire stream.

choices.text: those

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, what two specific TMIs are named as alternatives when DCT instability prevents normal metering-time compliance?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b1, miles-in-trail (MIT) and minutes-in-trail (MINIT) are named as TMIs that may be used between affected aircraft to assist delay absorption until stability resumes.

choices.text: Ground stops and ground delay programs.
Miles-in-trail (MIT) and minutes-in-trail (MINIT).
Airspace flow programs and reroutes.
Speed control and holding patterns.

items.question_text: Under 11-1-3, a controller who cannot meet a TBFM metering time should stop displaying the TBFM schedule and manage the sector without reference to TBFM until notification reaches TMU.
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3a and c, personnel must display TBFM schedule information on the situation display during metering periods, and when compliance is not possible they must coordinate with OS/CIC, traffic management services, and adjacent facilities/sectors. The schedule display is maintained regardless of compliance difficulties.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Under what authority can an ARTCC sector be exempted from the plus-or-minus-1-minute metering compliance standard under 11-1-3?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b2, an exception to the plus-or-minus-1-minute requirement may be authorized for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive. The exception is not tied to shift, weather, or controller discretion.

choices.text: Whenever the TMU determines conditions warrant a wider tolerance for the current shift.
During convective weather events when controllers report that plus-or-minus-1-minute compliance is impracticable.
Only if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive.
At the discretion of the sector controller whenever metering accuracy is in question.

items.situation: A facility is under scrutiny because its operational performance metrics show a pattern of applying miles-in-trail restrictions even during periods when TBFM is fully operational. When challenged, the traffic management unit says TBFM is treated as just one option among many equivalent tools, and MIT is often chosen because it requires less monitoring.
items.question_text: Is the facility's approach consistent with 11-1-1?

choices.text: Yes. The order permits traffic managers to select whichever flow tool they consider appropriate for the situation.
No. TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT. Treating both as equal options is not authorized.
Yes, because MIT is a simpler tool that reduces operational complexity relative to TBFM.
Yes, as long as the facility documents why TBFM was not chosen in each instance.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-1b, TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT. This is a mandatory preference, not an equal-option framework. Choosing MIT when TBFM is feasible violates the order.

items.question_text: An STMCIC must ensure an operational briefing is conducted. Which of the following accurately reflects the minimum who, when, and what requirements?

choices.text: Once per day shift only, with TMU personnel, covering TBM operations and TMIs.
At least once during day and evening shifts, with STMCIC, OSMIC/CIC and other designated personnel, covering weather, staffing, equipment, runways, AAR, TBM use, and TMIs.
Once per shift (day, evening, mid), with all controllers on duty, covering all operational topics.
As needed, with whichever supervisors are available, covering only topics that have changed since the previous briefing.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a1, the STMCIC ensures the briefing at least once during day and evening shifts, with minimum participants STMCIC, OSMIC/CIC, and other designated personnel, covering all the listed operational topics.

items.situation: An EDST outage occurs during a period of active metering. The STMCIC is unsure whether any specific actions are required beyond notifying the TMU. The OS/CIC asks whether controllers on position also have outage responsibilities.
items.question_text: Under 11-1-2, who bears EDST outage responsibilities and what governs those actions?

choices.text: Only the STMCIC has EDST outage responsibilities; other personnel should continue normal operations.
Only the TMU must act during an EDST outage; air traffic personnel are not involved.
The STMCIC, OS/CIC, and personnel providing air traffic services all have assigned EDST outage actions, governed by JO 7210.3 and the facility directive.
EDST outage actions are optional and left to the discretion of the facility manager.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a5, b6, and c5, the STMCIC, OS/CIC, and personnel providing air traffic services each must perform assigned actions in the event of an EDST outage or degradation, in accordance with JO 7210.3 and facility directive.

items.situation: During a shift, a controller at a TRACON notices that the current departure scheduling arrangement with the overlying ARTCC is causing gridlock because the written procedures are out of date. The controller proposes handling departures on a first-come-first-served basis for the rest of the shift to keep traffic moving.
items.question_text: How should the TRACON handle internal departure scheduling under these conditions?

choices.text: Switch to a first-come-first-served approach because the existing written procedures are not working.
Continue scheduling internal departures in accordance with the specific written procedures and agreements; coordinate for revisions if the current procedures are creating problems.
Suspend all internal departure scheduling until new written procedures can be developed.
Delegate departure scheduling entirely to the ATCT because the TRACON procedure is not functioning.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2e3, TRACONs must schedule internal departures in accordance with specific written procedures and agreements. Deviating from those procedures without coordination violates the requirement.

items.question_text: Which of the following correctly describes when TBFM schedule information must be displayed during metering?

choices.text: Only when the controller determines the information is operationally useful for the current sector.
Only when the plus-or-minus-1-minute standard can actually be met for all aircraft in the sector.
Always on the situation display during periods of metering, regardless of compliance difficulties.
On any available display, including the supervisor console or paper strips, whichever is most convenient.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-3a, during periods of metering, personnel must display TBFM schedule information on the situation display. This requirement is not conditional on compliance ability.

items.situation: A controller at a center sector has been told by their facility that an exception to the plus-or-minus-1-minute metering standard has been approved for their sector. When another controller from a different center asks where this exception is documented, the controller says it was a verbal authorization from the previous STMCIC.
items.question_text: Is this exception valid under 11-1-3?

choices.text: Yes, as long as the STMCIC documented the verbal authorization in a shift log.
No. An exception must be explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive — a verbal authorization is insufficient.
Yes, because STMCIC authorizations carry the force of a facility directive during the current shift.
No, but only because the authorization must come from the TMU rather than the STMCIC.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b2, an exception to the plus-or-minus-1-minute requirement may be authorized for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive. A verbal STMCIC authorization does not satisfy the 'explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive' requirement.
